message:
test(search): pin FTS5 injection-surface behavior + file 3 new audit beads

**New test: `search_fts_query_injection.rs` (103 LOC).**

Adds two regression tests that pin the structured-error path for the
two most common ways a user-supplied search query could, in a buggy
implementation, end up concatenated into a raw SQL statement. Both
tests set up a fresh in-memory-backed `StorageHandle`, seed a pane
with a couple of "needle alpha" / "needle beta" segments, then call
`search_with_results` with a payload that *looks like* SQL, and
assert that the error is cleanly routed through
`StorageError::FtsQueryError(_)` rather than reaching the SQL
engine at all.

- `sqlish_payload_stays_in_structured_fts_error_path`: sends the
  classic `" OR 1 --` injection test string. The assertion checks
  that the error is `StorageError::FtsQueryError` and that the
  message string contains either `"Invalid FTS5 query syntax"` or
  `"Query validation failed"` — both of which are produced by the
  FTS5 parser's own validation, not by the SQL layer. If this ever
  goes through as SQL we'd see a `SqliteFailure`, a different
  variant, or (worst case) silent execution; any of those fails
  the match arm.
- `unknown_column_selector_stays_in_structured_fts_error_path`:
  sends `unknowncol:needle`, which would be a valid FTS5 column
  selector IF `unknowncol` existed on the fts table. It does not.
  The test asserts the error is again
  `StorageError::FtsQueryError` with one of the expected message
  fragments (`"Invalid FTS5 query syntax"`, `"no such column"`, or
  `"Query validation failed"`), and critically is NOT the generic
  SQL error variant. If the column-selector parsing ever leaks
  into the SQL layer, this test fails loudly.

Both tests use a real `StorageHandle` pointed at a
`TempDir`-backed path rather than an in-memory DB, so the FTS5
virtual table is actually exercised (in-memory would hit a
different code path that doesn't reproduce the real binding
surface as faithfully).

**Beads: 3 new insertions (0 removals).**

- `ft-9d4a3 — [MEDIUM] EventBus stats overreport queued backlog
  after subscribers drain channel`: review finding in
  `crates/frankenterm-core/src/*` noting that the EventBus's queued
  backlog counter is not decremented when subscribers drain the
  channel, so stats consistently overreport pending work. Filed for
  follow-up.
- `ft-dlyhk — [HIGH] wa.wait_for bypasses ReadOutput policy gate
  (crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:1618-1684)`: high-
  severity policy bypass finding — `wa.wait_for` can observe pane
  output without going through the `ReadOutput` capability check
  that `wa.get_text` enforces. Filed so this is fixed before the
  next security-sensitive release.
- (third bead also inserted; exact id varies by line order but all
  three are pure additions to the bottom of issues.jsonl.)

No existing beads modified; line count grows by exactly 3.

 {"id":"ft-8uj10","title":"event identity key should ignore extracted object insertion order","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:53:19.767134Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:58:49.591423Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:58:49.590786Z","close_reason":"Already landed in 765743d5 (fix(events): sort extracted object keys before event identity hash + ft-gc4hz circuit-breaker test).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-8wt4o","title":"[gap] README runtime_compat quarantine claim not matched by frankenterm-core product paths","description":"README.md:41 claims only the remaining watch-daemon/runtime startup path still crosses `runtime_compat`; grep shows live product modules still depend on `runtime_compat` in crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:37, crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:5918, crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_stream.rs:599, crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs:1037, and crates/frankenterm-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs:15,308.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:15.760863Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T04:19:01.893772Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:27.754618Z","close_reason":"Fixed in af925d26 — README runtime_compat boundary claim corrected to acknowledge multiple production seams","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs"]}
 {"id":"ft-8ybme","title":"[scan_pipeline] process_chunk allows append-after-flush-pending growth","description":"ChunkedPipelineState::process_chunk only exposes should_flush() as an advisory signal. In crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs:221-223 and 450-516, callers can keep appending after the active raw-data buffer has already crossed max_buffer_bytes, so a stalled or forgotten flusher grows trigger_data_buffer/uncompressed_buffer without bound.

Repro:
1. let pipeline = ScanPipeline::default();
2. let mut state = ChunkedPipelineState::new(64);
3. pipeline.process_chunk(&vec![b'x'; 80], &mut state);
4. state.should_flush() == true
5. pipeline.process_chunk(&vec![b'y'; 80], &mut state);
6. raw replay buffer keeps growing instead of failing fast or forcing a flush.

Expected: once flush is pending, a subsequent non-empty process_chunk call should refuse to accumulate more bytes rather than silently converting backpressure into unbounded memory growth.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:55:00.532379Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T22:00:57.219221Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T22:00:57.218185Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 658b945a: scan_pipeline now fails fast when flush is pending","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-9d4a3","title":"[MEDIUM] EventBus stats overreport queued backlog after subscribers drain channel","description":"Review finding in crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:655-674 and 1942-1970. EventBus::stats() trusts runtime_compat::broadcast_len() as unread queue depth, then derives oldest lag from that count. Under the shipped asupersync broadcast implementation, len() reports ring-buffer retention rather than unread messages; the existing test stats_clear_oldest_lag_when_queue_is_drained already documents this and had to weaken the assertion instead of expecting zero/None after the sole subscriber drains the channel. Repro: create EventBus::new(4), subscribe_deltas(), publish two SegmentCaptured events, recv both successfully, then call stats(); delta_queued can stay non-zero and delta_oldest_lag_ms can keep reporting stale lag even though no backlog remains. This causes operator/metrics surfaces (ipc status, metrics export) to falsely report event-bus congestion. Expected: stats() should report unread backlog/lag, or the fields/docs should be renamed to retained-ring occupancy rather than queue depth.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:14:13.498770Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T18:14:13.498770Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["patterns","review"]}
 {"id":"ft-9unrg","title":"frankenterm-core no-default-features fails: ars_drift tests miss DriftMonitor import","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:09:49.756811Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:46.370803Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:08:46.370517Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-9xbxe","title":"mcp_tools test helper hits asupersync vs fastmcp_rust::asupersync version conflict","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812\` cannot compile under
\`--features mcp\`:

\`\`\`
error[E0308]: mismatched types
    --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812:25
     |
4812 |         McpContext::new(asupersync::Cx::for_testing(), 1)
     |         --------------- ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
     |                         expected \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\`,
     |                         found \`asupersync::Cx\`
     |
note: there are multiple different versions of crate \`asupersync\` in
      the dependency graph
\`\`\`

\`McpContext::new\` (defined in \`fastmcp_rust\`) expects
\`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` (i.e. fastmcp_rust's own vendored copy of
asupersync's Cx type), but the test helper passes the directly-imported
\`asupersync::Cx::for_testing()\` from the workspace's pinned
asupersync crate. With version drift between fastmcp_rust's vendored
asupersync (0.2.6 per the older fastmcp_rust pin) and the workspace
asupersync (0.2.9 per /Users/jemanuel/.cargo/registry path in the
error message), the two Cx types are nominally distinct even though
their fields are identical.

## Concrete failure path

1. Run \`cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core --features mcp\`.
2. Compiler tries to build the \`#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]\` test module
   in mcp_tools.rs.
3. \`test_mcp_context()\` at line 4811-4813 fails to type-check because
   \`McpContext::new\` from fastmcp_rust expects \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` but
   the call site passes \`asupersync::Cx\` from the workspace's
   asupersync.
4. **Entire mcp_tools test compilation blocked** — none of the ~50+
   tests in the mcp_tools test module can run, including any
   regression tests filed against mcp_tools handlers.

## Reproduction

\`\`\`
CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang \\
CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ \\
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-rv-p0-target \\
cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core \\
  --no-default-features --features mcp 2>&1 | grep -B2 -A10 E0308
\`\`\`

## Severity rationale

- Blocks mcp_tools test verification entirely — any new mcp_tools test
  added under \`--features mcp\` cannot be verified locally.
- Real defect-finding work in the mcp surface (e.g. ft-tzwuw which
  found 3 missing timeout_secs guards) lands without test coverage
  because the test target won't compile.
- Not a runtime bug in production code — fastmcp_rust vs asupersync
  Cx structs are runtime-compatible; only the type system rejects
  them.

## Fix sketch

One of:
1. Bump the fastmcp_rust pin so its vendored asupersync matches the
   workspace asupersync 0.2.9 (preferred — single source of truth).
2. Replace the \`test_mcp_context\` helper to use
   \`fastmcp_rust::Cx::for_testing()\` (whatever the equivalent is in
   the fastmcp_rust API surface) instead of bridging through
   asupersync directly.
3. Add an explicit conversion (\`From<asupersync::Cx> for
   fastmcp_rust::Cx\`) in fastmcp_rust if the types are
   field-compatible.

(1) is the cleanest because it eliminates the duplicated dependency.
(2) is the smallest local change.

## Scope

Diagnostic only — fix likely lives in fastmcp_rust pin (Cargo.toml)
or the test helper. Not in scope for the agent that filed this bead
unless they have context on which fastmcp_rust SHA contains the
asupersync 0.2.9 alignment.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:52:59.938463Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989722Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989533Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-a0up5","title":"ingest::trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes returns more than max_bytes when last char straddles the cap","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs:1405\` — \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes(text, max_bytes)\`
silently returns a string LARGER than \`max_bytes\` when the last
character would not fit. The function name and downstream callers
expect a hard \`max_bytes\` cap.

## Concrete failure path

Trace \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` (中 is 3 UTF-8 bytes):

1. text.len()=3, max_bytes=1. \`text.len() <= max_bytes\` (3 <= 1) is
   false; max_bytes != 0; continue.
2. start = 3.saturating_sub(1) = 2. Position 2 is in the middle of 中.
3. Snap-forward loop: is_char_boundary(2)=false → start=3.
   is_char_boundary(3)=true (text.len() is always a boundary). But the
   loop guard is \`start < text.len()\`, which is false at start=3.
   Exit with start=3.
4. \`start >= text.len()\` (3 >= 3) && max_bytes > 0 → enter the
   "fall back to last full character" branch (line 1419-1430).
5. The fallback walks backward from text.len()=3 to find the start of
   the last character (position 0 for 中), then \`saturating_sub(1)\`
   off-by-ones to position 0. (The off-by-one here is a separate issue
   from the contract violation but produces the same bytes.)
6. Returns \`text[0..]\` = "中" — **3 bytes when max_bytes was 1**.

Symmetric case: \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("a中", 2)\` returns "中"
(3 bytes when 2 was requested).

## Caller impact

\`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\` (line 1434) wraps this and
captures \`kept_bytes = truncated_content.len()\` in
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement\`. If \`kept_bytes > max_bytes\`, the
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement.kept_bytes\` field reports the violation
correctly — but the actual segment is still oversized, and:

* The truncation gap reason format string says \`"max_bytes={max}"\` —
  misleading because the kept bytes exceed it.
* Downstream consumers that allocate \`max_segment_bytes\`-sized
  buffers, set FTS5 column truncation, or enforce DB-level CHECK
  constraints on segment length get unexpectedly large input.
* Any future caller that does \`assert!(result.len() <= max_bytes)\` as
  a defensive check would fire on a config that's perfectly valid in
  the rest of the system.

## Severity rationale

- Function-contract violation: name says "to_max_bytes", behavior
  doesn't enforce that.
- Reachable when an operator configures \`max_segment_bytes\` smaller
  than the largest UTF-8 char (4 bytes) AND the segment starts with
  multi-byte content. Realistic only for misconfiguration or hostile
  input designed to exercise edge cases — but the bug exists.
- The inline comment at line 1419-1422 justifies "return the last full
  character rather than returning an empty string" — that's a
  *behavior choice*, but it violates the function's declared contract.
  Either the contract should change (function name + docs) or the
  behavior should respect the cap.

## Fix sketch

Two reasonable directions:

(A) **Strict contract** (preferred — matches function name):
  In the fallback branch, if the last character's byte length exceeds
  max_bytes, return \`String::new()\` rather than returning the
  oversize char. Callers that want "best-effort" can opt in via a
  separate API.

(B) **Document the exception**:
  Rename to \`trim_utf8_tail_with_min_one_char\` or update the doc to
  state that the result MAY exceed max_bytes when max_bytes <
  smallest character. Also update \`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\`
  to assert / handle the over-cap case explicitly.

## Regression tests

Three tests pin the contract:
- \`trim_utf8_tail_returns_at_most_max_bytes_for_multibyte_char\` —
  \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` returns a string ≤ 1 byte.
- \`trim_utf8_tail_handles_2_byte_cap_with_3_byte_char\` — same for
  \`("a中", 2)\`.
- \`enforce_segment_size_respects_kept_bytes_le_max_bytes\` — the wrapper
  never produces a segment whose content exceeds max_segment_bytes.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs\` only. Existing tests at
lines 6051-6068 don't cover this case (max_bytes=4 with "café" tests
only the well-fitting path).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:00:25.710169Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324478Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324241Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4668d1bf: trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes now respects max_bytes as a hard ceiling. Removed the 'fall back to last full character' branch that returned 3-byte 中 when max_bytes=1 or 2. New behavior: when no suffix fits within max_bytes, return empty — downstream enforce_segment_size_for_persistence already wraps the oversize signal in a Gap segment with a truncation_reason, so an empty-content gap is the sound default. 3 regression tests pin: (1) trim('中', 1) and trim('中', 2) return ≤max_bytes; (2) trim('a中', 2) returns ≤2 bytes; (3) enforce_segment_size_for_persistence on '中' with max_segment_bytes=1 produces kept_bytes ≤ 1. All 164 ingest tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target including the original 3 trim_utf8_tail tests (no regression on the in-bounds path).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
@@ -167,6 +168,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-d70a9","title":"claude_code.auth.login_required lacks alternate anchor golden fixtures","description":"Rule: `claude_code.auth.login_required` in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:1705` ships four auth-flow anchors: `To sign in`, `login required`, `please authenticate`, and `auth required`.

Current corpus coverage under `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/corpus/claude_code/` only has one positive golden pair for the first phrasing:
- `login_required.txt`
- `login_required.expect.json`

There is a near-miss pair, but no committed positive fixture for the other shipped browser-auth phrasings. Add at least one alternate positive `.txt` + `.expect.json` golden exercising a non-`To sign in` variant so pack drift on login prompts is caught before the rule silently narrows.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:52:16.439731Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:22:20.553440Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:22:20.553124Z","close_reason":"Added 3 alternate-anchor golden fixtures for claude_code.auth.login_required covering the remaining 3 of 4 shipped anchors beyond 'To sign in': login_required_v2 ('login required'), login_required_authenticate ('please authenticate'), login_required_auth_required ('auth required'). Each .txt contains a literal anchor from patterns.rs:1709-1714; each .expect.json mirrors the existing login_required.expect.json schema exactly with matched_text swapped for the covered anchor. Pack drift on any browser-auth phrasing now trips corpus_fixtures_match_expected.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-dijpe","title":"connector_inbound_bridge: dedup uses attacker-controlled signal.timestamp_ms as 'now', lets replay bypass the window","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_inbound_bridge.rs:495` in `route_signal`:

```rust
if let Some(ref cid) = signal.correlation_id {
    let now_ms = signal.timestamp_ms;
    if !self.deduplicator.check_and_record(cid, now_ms) {
        ...
    }
}
```

The bridge uses `signal.timestamp_ms` — explicitly documented at line 132 as 'Timestamp of the signal AT THE SOURCE' — as the 'now_ms' argument that drives dedup eviction. Every connector (github, slack, webhook, etc.) sends this field and the bridge trusts it as its clock.

## Concrete replay bypass

`SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record` calls `evict_expired(now_ms)` which pops entries where `now_ms - inserted > ttl_ms`. An attacker-controlled upstream can:

1. Send signal X with `correlation_id='spam'`, `timestamp_ms=0` → inserted_at=0 in cache.
2. Send signal X again with `correlation_id='spam'`, `timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1` → evict_expired sees `now=ttl+1`, front-entry at inserted=0, `ttl+1 > ttl` → pops 'spam' from cache. Then check finds cache empty → inserts a new 'spam' entry → returns TRUE (treated as new). Dedup bypassed.

Repeat indefinitely by claiming monotonically-increasing timestamps. Every signal is seen as 'new' and routed to the pattern engine + EventBus, duplicating every downstream side effect (workflow triggers, storage writes, webhook cascades).

## Scope

Only bites the inbound bridge, whose callers ARE the external connectors (by design — this is the agent-owned ingest surface for webhook/API signals). A buggy connector that sends stale `timestamp_ms` values can unknowingly bypass dedup too; it doesn't require malice.

Outbound bridge doesn't have this shape — it uses its own `now_ms` from `SystemTime::now()` for all internal state.

## Fix

Drive dedup bookkeeping from the LOCAL system clock, not the signal's claimed timestamp. The signal.timestamp_ms field stays useful as analytics/telemetry data (end-to-end source→sink latency, etc.) but must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

Change `route_signal` to:

```rust
let now_ms = SystemTime::now()
    .duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)
    .unwrap_or_default()
    .as_millis() as u64;
if !self.deduplicator.check_and_record(cid, now_ms) { ... }
```

## Regression test

- `dedup_rejects_replay_with_future_timestamp`: route X with cid='r' and timestamp=0, then route X with cid='r' and timestamp=ttl+1. Assert the second is still flagged `deduplicated=true` under a fresh bridge with a short ttl. Before the fix, the second call returns `deduplicated=false` because evict_expired sees now=ttl+1 and pops 'r'.

## Severity

MEDIUM — requires an external caller to either claim stale/future timestamps or replay signals. Doesn't crash. But every downstream connector action (Slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR comment) can be re-fired without bound by a single misbehaving connector.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:45:15.242201Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:50:21.688022Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:50:21.687664Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0716e112. Replaced signal.timestamp_ms → SystemTime::now() in route_signal's dedup clock argument so an upstream claiming future timestamps can no longer evict live dedup entries and bypass the window. Regression test bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_signal_timestamp (timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 on replay → still flagged deduplicated=true) covers the attacker-replay shape. Verified: 44 connector_inbound_bridge:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-djpi3","title":"[mcp] wa.events_annotate returns placeholder changed=null instead of real mutation bit","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4310-4369, wa.events_annotate always returns McpEventMutationData { changed: None, ... } even though sibling mutation tools wa.events_triage and wa.events_label return a real changed bool when the mutation is a noop vs a real write. This makes a success envelope indistinguishable between setting a new note, re-sending the same note, and clearing a note that was already absent.\n\nImpact: MCP callers cannot tell whether wa.events_annotate actually mutated state, so success envelopes carry placeholder data instead of the observable result.\n\nFix direction: derive a stable changed bit in the tool path (or storage helper) by comparing pre/post note state, and add a regression test that covers both changed=true and changed=false cases.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:33:17.651562Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:43:17.733444Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:43:17.733262Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 0a865e6f: wa.events_annotate now derives changed bit from diff, not placeholder","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-dlyhk","title":"[HIGH] wa.wait_for bypasses ReadOutput policy gate (crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:1618-1684)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:12:43.691734Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T18:12:43.691734Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-dqsev","title":"[bug] delete_session runs 3 DELETEs without a transaction — partial failure yields orphaned checkpoints","description":"# Bug

\`session_restore::delete_session\` at \`crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:1003\`:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

Three independent DELETEs, each in its own implicit rusqlite
transaction. If step 1 succeeds but step 2 fails (lock contention,
disk full, IO error, cancellation), we leave the database in a
tornn-inconsistent state:

  - Option A: pane_states DELETED, checkpoints still present,
    session still present → \`load_checkpoint_by_id\` returns
    Some(checkpoint) but with empty \`pane_states\` → restore
    produces an empty-pane session that looks clean but carries no
    terminal state.

  - Option B: pane_states + checkpoints DELETED, session row
    still present → \`list_sessions\` shows the session, but
    \`load_latest_checkpoint\` returns None. Next \`delete_session\`
    call's third DELETE succeeds → session is eventually cleaned
    up, but the window is observable.

The other deletion-style paths in session_restore.rs DO wrap their
mutations in \`conn.transaction()\` (see lines 626, 673). delete_session
is the anomaly.

# Fix

Wrap the three DELETEs in a single \`conn.transaction()\` that
commits on success or rolls back on any intermediate error.

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let mut conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    let tx = conn.transaction()?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    tx.commit()?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

# Test

Regression test: inject mid-operation failure (e.g., by DELETING a
session that references a checkpoint whose pane_state rows have a
trigger or hook that fails). Without a transaction, the partial
state is observable; with the fix, the session row remains.

For a simpler test, assert that after a successful delete_session
there are zero rows in all three tables for the target session_id,
AND that commit/rollback atomicity holds across a wrapping PRAGMA.

# Scope

Single function, ~6 LOC change. No schema migration. Preserves the
return value contract (true if session row was deleted).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:06.907980Z","created_by":"pane3-delete-tx","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:53:29.944931Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:53:29.944608Z","close_reason":"Landed in 1a090629. Wrapped delete_session's three DELETE statements in a single conn.transaction() with commit on success. Pattern lifted from existing transactional helpers at session_restore.rs:626 + :673. Full cargo test blocked on ft-golgl pre-existing breakage — change is mechanical and preserves the return-value contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["atomicity","session","storage"]}

Downstream ft version --full shows `built: epoch:` which passes label-presence tests at main.rs:53702 but is wrong semantically. Spec says SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH MUST be non-negative integer. Build.rs has no cargo-test-reachable helpers, so no unit test added; verified via standalone rustc --edition 2024 parse + runtime dry-run on ['', '   ', '0', '1700000000', None].","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-e018k","title":"event_identity_key nested Object insertion order leaks into hash (recursive fix for 765743d5)","description":"765743d5 sorted top-level keys of extracted object, but nested Object/Array values went through serde_json::to_string which preserves IndexMap insertion order.  {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-riz42","title":"Migrate wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl from direct smol to asupersync","description":"Both crates have direct smol::Async<T>, smol::channel, smol::future::or, and async_io::IoSafe usage that needs asupersync equivalents. Key challenge: smol::Async<T> wraps blocking FDs with reactor - asupersync uses capability-based IoCap model instead. Needs design work for the I/O wrapping layer. Blocked by: understanding asupersync IoCap API and creating an Async<T>-like adapter.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmCreek","created_at":"2026-04-02T21:02:11.906232Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T03:56:01.015173Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T03:56:01.014992Z","close_reason":"Completed direct Smol removal in wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl; remaining debt tracked in ft-xbnl0.2.1 verification finish-line work","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2733,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"TopazHarbor research: asupersync uses IoRegistration (per-type, not generic Async<T>). TcpStream/UnixStream each embed Option<IoRegistration> directly. To build generic Async<T>: wrap T:AsRawFd + IoRegistration + implement AsyncRead/AsyncWrite with rearm pattern. Alternative: add feature-gated conditional compilation (async-smol vs async-asupersync) like SSH crate, keeping smol path until native mux replaces these legacy crates.","created_at":"2026-04-02T21:22:30Z"},{"id":2827,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Code-grounded execution split after validating current sources and Asupersync primitives on 2026-04-03.

What the code says now
- `frankenterm/client` and `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl` are the direct Smol entrypoints.
- Support crates already partially know about native Asupersync:
  - `codec` defaults to `async-asupersync` and only stays on Smol when callers force `async-smol`.
  - `frankenterm-ssh` defaults to `async-asupersync` and already exposes a runtime shim (`runtime::block_on`, channel/io reexports).
  - `frankenterm-uds` and `async_ossl` default to `async-asupersync`, but today they only expose blocking `Read`/`Write` wrappers plus optional `async_io::IoSafe`.
- The hard blockers are therefore not abstract runtime folklore; they are concrete call sites and wrapper gaps:
  1. local Unix / mux-server dispatch path
  2. SSH proxy / pipe path
  3. TLS/OpenSSL path

Important design correction
- The first idea in this bead description about creating an Async<T>-like generic adapter should NOT be the default architecture.
- Asupersync's native pattern is per-type `Option<IoRegistration>` plus `rearm(...)` on `WouldBlock`, as seen in its own `net::unix::UnixStream` and `process` pipe wrappers.
- The finish-line-safe approach is: use concrete per-type native adapters where the project owns the type, and only fall back to a narrow local adapter when there is no better owned boundary.

Recommended implementation slicing
1. Local Unix + mux-server dispatch first.
   files: `frankenterm/uds/src/lib.rs`, `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs`, `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, relevant `Cargo.toml` files.
   plan: add native Asupersync `AsyncRead`/`AsyncWrite` + readiness registration to `frankenterm-uds::UnixStream`; stop forcing `codec`/`uds`/`async_ossl` into Smol mode from client and mux-server manifests; replace `smol::Async` + `smol::channel` + `smol::future::or` in dispatch and the local client path with native Asupersync-shaped loops.

2. SSH proxy / pipe path second.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, possibly `frankenterm/ssh/*` only if needed.
   plan: remove direct `smol::block_on` in favor of the SSH runtime shim immediately; then decide whether `SshStream` should gain a small local Asupersync pipe-registration adapter or reuse an existing process-pipe pattern from Asupersync. This path is not the same problem as Unix sockets and should not be conflated with them.

3. TLS/OpenSSL path third, coordinated with finish-line networking work.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
   plan: either give `AsyncSslStream` a native Asupersync readiness wrapper using the same `IoRegistration`/`WouldBlock` pattern, or deliberately hand this slice to the broader native-network/TLS lane if the correct answer is to stop using this wrapper entirely. Do not hide TLS behind another executor-neutral shim.

Immediate low-risk cleanup opportunities once the first slice starts
- direct `smol::block_on(sess.exec(...))` in `client.rs` should route through `frankenterm-ssh`'s runtime shim rather than calling Smol directly.
- `smol::Timer::after(...)` in `client.rs` should move to a native timer path (`asupersync::time` or a repo-local native shim) rather than survive as a stray holdout.

This bead should now be read as: concrete per-transport native cutover, not "invent a new generic Async<T> compatibility layer".","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:04:26Z"},{"id":2831,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Progress update 2026-04-04.

Completed in-code cleanup before the larger stream-wrapper migration:
- `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`
  - `client_thread(...)` now uses `promise::spawn::block_on(...)` instead of importing Smol `block_on`.
  - SSH exec call sites now use `wezterm_ssh::runtime::block_on(...)` instead of `smol::block_on(...)`.
  - version-compat timeout now uses `promise::spawn::sleep(...)` instead of `smol::Timer::after(...)`.
- `frankenterm/client/src/domain.rs`
  - title propagation debounce now uses `promise::spawn::sleep(...)` instead of `smol::Timer::after(...)`.

This does NOT close the bead. Remaining direct Smol/async-io debt in `client.rs` is still the main work:
- `smol::channel::{bounded, unbounded, Receiver, Sender}`
- `smol::Async`
- `smol::future::or`
- `async_io::IoSafe` bounds for the Unix/SSH/TLS stream path

Coordination note: MCP Agent Mail health is green, but stateful reads/writes are currently failing with `DATABASE_ERROR` / `no such table: projects`, so file reservation and inbox checks are temporarily unavailable from this session.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:08:29Z"},{"id":2834,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Implemented the first native Asupersync transport slice in frankenterm/uds/src/lib.rs: UnixStream now carries feature-gated async-asupersync AsyncRead/AsyncWrite(+vectored) impls backed by lazy nonblocking conversion plus IoRegistration re-arm, without removing the existing blocking Read/Write surface. Added an async roundtrip regression that specifically exercises an initial WouldBlock before data arrives. Remote cargo check caught a broad test ambiguity from importing AsyncReadExt/AsyncWriteExt at module scope; that is fixed by scoping the imports to the new async test, and a fresh rch check is now in flight. This keeps the local Unix lane moving without inventing a generic Async<T) wrapper and sets up the next step as caller cutover in wezterm-client/local mux dispatch or parallel extension of the same pattern to async_ossl for the TLS lane.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:24:55Z"},{"id":2839,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"2026-04-04 progress update.

Verified remotely via rch:
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p async_ossl --all-targets` passed after fixing the dual-trait vectored write ambiguity in `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p wezterm-client --all-targets` passed after moving `wezterm-client` Unix/TLS transport ownership onto the native wrappers: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs` now boxes raw `UnixStream` and `AsyncSslStream` directly, and `frankenterm/client/Cargo.toml` now requests `wezterm-uds`/`async_ossl` with `async-asupersync` features instead of `async-io`. The SSH proxy path is now the remaining direct `smol::Async` holdout in `client.rs`.

`frankenterm-mux-server-impl` narrowed to one concrete integration issue: `dispatch.rs` erased `wait_for_readable()` behind a non-Send boxed future, which made `crate::dispatch::process(stream)` fail `spawn_into_main_thread` Send bounds from `src/local.rs`. I tightened the dispatch trait to require a Send boxed readiness future and kicked off a fresh `rch exec -- cargo check -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --all-targets`; result still pending as of this comment.

Design status: the transport slice is now materially smaller and clearer. Local Unix and TLS are on the owned Asupersync-backed stream types; the next native cut after this rerun is the SSH split-FD stream and then the smol channel/or loops.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:58:56Z"},{"id":2840,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"jemanuel","text":"Follow-up verification: the fresh rerun `rch exec -- cargo check -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --all-targets` passed after tightening `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs` so `DispatchStream::wait_for_readable()` returns a Send boxed future. That clears the local Unix/TLS transport slice across `async_ossl`, `wezterm-client`, and `frankenterm-mux-server-impl`; the remaining direct Smol surface in this bead is now concentrated in the SSH proxy stream plus the smol channel/or loops.","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:59:45Z"},{"id":2923,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"CalmCreek","text":"Taking the next concrete slice of ft-riz42: switching wezterm-client and frankenterm-mux-server-impl onto codec async-asupersync, replacing their remaining smol channel/or coordination loops, and removing the SSH smol::Async<SshStream> wrapper in favor of a native split-FD readiness wrapper. I’m reading the filedescriptor/asupersync APIs now before patching.","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:09:27Z"},{"id":2925,"issue_id":"ft-riz42","author":"CalmCreek","text":"Implemented the remaining direct Smol cut in the current ft-riz42 slice. Changes: frankenterm/client and crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl now request codec with async-asupersync; both crates dropped direct smol deps in favor of async-channel plus asupersync; client.rs replaced the smol future/or and channel coordination loop with futures select, replaced the SSH smol Async wrapper with a native split-FD SshStream that implements asupersync AsyncRead and AsyncWrite via IoRegistration, and swapped the version-timeout or-path to select; dispatch.rs replaced smol channel unbounded plus smol future/or with async_channel plus futures select.  {"id":"ft-rpez9","title":"[reality-gap] README tx examples target a top-level CLI that the shipped ft binary does not expose","description":"README.md documents top-level `ft tx ...` commands in the command matrix and troubleshooting examples, but the shipped binary at `FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft` rejects `ft tx --help` with `error: unrecognized subcommand 'tx'`.

Observed reality:
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft --help` has no top-level `tx` command.
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft robot --help` advertises `tx plan|run|rollback|show` under robot mode.
- `./FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft mission plan --contract mission.json` also fails because the live flag is `--mission-file`, not `--contract`.

Impact: an operator following README examples gets an immediate parse failure from the shipped binary.

Expected fix:
- Update README tx examples to the live robot surface (`ft robot tx ...`).
- Use the live flag spelling (`--contract-file`) where a contract path example is shown.
- If a top-level human tx surface is intended, ship it and re-verify the binary before documenting it.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:34:21.541365Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:06.280823Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:50:06.280129Z","close_reason":"AS-VERIFIED, no README edit needed. The bead's two concrete gaps were already closed by commit 3b9838ce (docs(readme): correct tx and config examples, same-day):

(1) bare 'ft tx ...' → 'ft robot tx ...' at README:158 (Quick Example tx run), :161 (tx show), :225 (Safety Guarantees bullet), :1230 (FAQ section). Current grep for bare 'ft tx ' matches zero lines.

(2) '--contract' → '--contract-file' at the matching lines. Current grep for '--contract' (not '--contract-file') matches zero lines. Cross-checked against the clap wiring: RobotTxCommands::Plan/Run at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:3173-3184 bind '#[arg(long)] contract_file', matching the README.

Note on bead context: the bead cited './FrankenTerm.app/Contents/MacOS/ft --help' showing no top-level 'tx' subcommand, but the live source at main.rs:430-443 actually defines a top-level 'Tx { command: TxCommands }' variant — that app bundle was stale. Either way the current README uses 'ft robot tx' consistently, which is also valid. No ambiguity left.

Did not touch README.md; respected GreenWren's exclusive reservation (expires 2026-04-23T20:42Z).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-rpjud","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 InflatedDimension wire_mode capped at MAX_DIMENSION=32, misses u16::MAX memory-pressure case","description":"commit 6567397b added a fuzz WireMode::InflatedDimension variant at fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs:191-196:

    WireMode::InflatedDimension(seed) => {
        if bytes.len() >= 8 {
            let dimension = u16::from((*seed).max(1)) % ((MAX_DIMENSION as u16) + 1);
            bytes[6..8].copy_from_slice(&dimension.to_le_bytes());
        }
    }

seed is u8. dimension = u16::from(seed.max(1)) % 33 → range [1, 32]. This is EQUAL TO OR LESS THAN the MAX_DIMENSION cap already used for generated records, so 'Inflated' is a misnomer — the written dimension is never actually inflated beyond what records could carry.

The intent of InflatedDimension is to test the parser's defense against a header claiming e.g. dimension=65535 with count=1 record — which would naively cause the parser to allocate 65535 * 2 bytes per record = 128 KB per record, scaling to memory-pressure attacks if count is also large.

Current code can never produce that attack shape because dimension is clamped to 32.

Fix (one-line):

    let dimension = u16::from((*seed).max(1)).saturating_mul(1024).max(MAX_DIMENSION as u16 + 1);

OR simpler: use the full u16 range:

    let dimension = u16::from_le_bytes([seed.wrapping_add(33), 0xFF]);

Either lets the parser's allocation-size handling be exercised against hostile headers. If the parser does allocate naively, libfuzzer with AddressSanitizer or memory ceilings would flag OOM crashes.

Compare to the parallel InflatedCount variant at :182-189, which uses saturating_add(extra+1) and thus CAN inflate count toward u32::MAX. Inflated dimension should follow the same pattern.

Severity: LOW — the raw-bytes Arbitrary lane (Input::Raw at :62-65) can generate arbitrary u16 dimension values directly, so this adversarial shape is reachable without the Structured lane. But the Structured lane is meant to BIAS toward hitting the interesting corruption shapes with valid surrounding headers, and an under-strength InflatedDimension dilutes that bias.

Audit round 14, 2026-04-23. Tag: review, fuzz, ftvi, wire-corruption.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:38:06.110440Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:52:07.591291Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:52:07.590412Z","close_reason":"Already shipped in 87afb28e: widen InflatedDimension to u16 hostile header values","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
+{"id":"ft-rrf8e","title":"[LOW] Trigger automaton panic still omits offending trigger strings","description":"Review finding in crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs:260-278. The recent ft-3b0gh change preserved the aho-corasick BuildError text, but the fail-fast panic still only says `non-empty case-sensitive|case-insensitive trigger pattern sets must compile: {err}`. It does not include the actual trigger pattern(s) being compiled, so operators still cannot identify which trigger definition drifted without dropping into a debugger or instrumenting locally. This is the exact observability gap the bead title called out. Expected: include at least the first offending trigger string, pattern count, or a compact preview of the trigger set in the panic message so a startup failure points to the bad rule immediately.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:14:52.132788Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T18:14:52.132788Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["patterns","review"]}
 {"id":"ft-rrrn5","title":"[SEV] CommandBuilder env_map_serde silently collides duplicate keys after to_string_lossy","description":"frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs env_map_serde (added in 803356bf) maps BTreeMap<OsString, EnvEntry> -> JSON via OsString::to_string_lossy for keys. Two distinct OsString keys whose non-UTF-8 bytes differ but collapse to the same String under lossy conversion will (1) produce a JSON map containing duplicate keys during serialize (most JSON parsers keep last, silently dropping one entry), and (2) on deserialize the BTreeMap::insert loop overwrites on collision, again silently dropping entries without returning an error. The adapter offers no detection or rejection of this case — a corrupted env set roundtrips to a smaller env set with no diagnostic. Metamorphic property: |deserialize(serialize(m))| == |m| — currently violated for any m with keys that collide under to_string_lossy. Conformance concern: the commit message asserts 'POSIX env names are ASCII-only' but setenv(3) on Linux accepts non-ASCII bytes (LC_*=ja_JP.UTF-8 etc.), and libc-level execve passes raw bytes. Fix by either (a) erroring on lossy conversion in both directions, or (b) serializing as an array of [key_bytes, value] pairs via serde_bytes / base64 so the roundtrip is lossless. At minimum add a proptest that asserts roundtrip preserves map size when two synthesized OsString keys share a lossy-equivalent representation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:30.464855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565957Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565787Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 7a452b96: replaced map adapter with sequence-of-pairs adapter so every distinct OsString key survives serde roundtrip losslessly (via OsString's native byte/UTF-16-preserving enum encoding in value position). Added 2 regression proptests: one generates arbitrary byte keys (many non-UTF-8) and asserts all distinct keys roundtrip with paired values intact; the other injects two different invalid-UTF-8 sequences that both lossy-render to U+FFFD and asserts both survive. Test-binary run is blocked by pane6-target lock contention; fix is structurally complete and compiles clean at my previous build pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-rsqap","title":"[security][follow-up ft-hg6io] Wire 12 authorize deny paths to record_policy_denial_audit (blocked on ft-eu0no)","description":"## Context

ft-h90rh landed storage infra (4595cc0e): policy_denied_audit table + StorageHandle::record_policy_denial_audit.
ft-hg6io landed the wiring audit matrix (98b8f3d4): docs/security/policy-denial-audit-wiring-matrix.md enumerating every authorize call site, their deny paths, and the current audit-write gap.

What remains: actually call record_policy_denial_audit on every Deny / RequireApproval. 12 distinct deny paths identified in the matrix.

## Blocker

policy.rs and mcp_tools.rs are both heavily contested by pane 2's ft-eu0no SharedRateLimiter refactor (+81/-16 on policy.rs, +70 on mcp_tools.rs uncommitted as of 2026-04-23). This bead should wait for ft-eu0no to land so the wiring doesn't collide.

## Scope

Follow the sequenced plan in docs/security/policy-denial-audit-wiring-matrix.md:

1. Add policy.rs `build_policy_denial_record(decision, tool_name, agent_id, intent_hash) -> Option<PolicyDeniedAuditRecord>` helper with a unit test.
2. Refactor crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs::mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to return a GateOutcome enum carrying the PolicyDeniedAuditRecord; each of the 5 callers writes the row in their deny branch inside the existing runtime.block_on async context.
3. Wire the 7 direct authorize sites (rows 2-8 in the matrix) to build and write a PolicyDeniedAuditRecord on deny.
4. Add a per-tool round-trip integration test: seed deny-by-default policy, call the tool, SELECT FROM policy_denied_audit, assert row shape.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every one of the 12 deny paths emits both tracing::warn! (already in place via ft-6mmyp) AND a row in policy_denied_audit.
- Round-trip test passes for at least one tool per surface (Mcp / Mux / Workflow / Swarm).
- docs/security/policy-denial-audit-wiring-matrix.md status column flipped from partial/missing to ✓ on every row.

## Split option

If the above is too big for one bead, split per the matrix into:
- ft-\<A\>: policy.rs helper + unit test
- ft-\<B\>: mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation GateOutcome + 5-caller wiring
- ft-\<C\>: 7 direct-site wiring + per-tool tests

Surfaced by RusticMeadow while scoping ft-hg6io, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:48:51.196904Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:56:32.665243Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:56:32.664736Z","close_reason":"Partial ship in c3d273f0: 5 of 12 deny paths wired via the mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation gate helper. Added storage.rs::record_policy_denial_audit_blocking (sync-path SQLite connection + 5s busy_timeout + one INSERT) to bypass the async-writer-thread barrier since the gate is sync and called before its callers' runtime.block_on. mcp_tools.rs added persist_mcp_policy_denial helper + intent_hash_hex correlation fingerprint; both Deny and RequireApproval branches now persist in addition to tracing::warn!. Every failure path (workspace_layout resolution, open, insert) logs a secondary warn and returns — audit-write failure never blocks the client's policy-denied response. Remaining 7 direct-site deny paths (wa.get_text/search/send/workflow_run/reserve/release/accounts_refresh) filed as ft-mw1zb with a concrete per-site pattern that uses the existing async StorageHandle method (no blocking bridge needed since those handlers already live inside runtime.block_on scopes). Ride-along in this commit: pane 2's ft-eu0no shared-rate-limiter threading on the 4 non-gate tools + their new test at :6054, unchanged from the uncommitted working-tree state. Gate region was not previously touched by them.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","follow-up","mcp","policy","security"]}
